notes.note: The letter-heading: "Cicero to Cassius, greetings." S. = salutem dicit, "sends greetings"; the recipient is C. Cassius Longinus, one of Caesar's assassins, now in the East raising republican armies.
M. Iunius Brutus, the other leading tyrannicide, consolidating his position in Macedonia and Greece; distinct from D. (Decimus) Brutus, named just after.

notes.anchor: my dear Dolabella has done so brilliantly
notes.note: As consul in 44 BC P. Cornelius Dolabella violently demolished the altar and column to Caesar that had sprung up in the Forum and dispersed the mob around it; Cicero applauds him here, before Dolabella turned to Caesarian and murdered Trebonius.

notes.anchor: we seem to have been liberated not from the kingship but from the king
notes.note: A play on rex (king) and regnum (kingship): the assassins removed the man (the dictator Caesar) but not his autocratic regime, whose acts continued to be enforced.

notes.anchor: forged decrees of the Senate are produced
notes.note: After Caesar's death Antony exploited Caesar's papers (the acta Caesaris), passing off edicts, grants, and recalls as the dead dictator's authentic intentions; Cicero charges that some were outright forgeries.

notes.anchor: Whose laws on bronze we ought to be tearing down
notes.note: Roman statutes and decrees were inscribed on bronze tablets posted in public; "tearing down" (refigere) the dead Caesar's tablets would have meant repudiating his measures, which the Senate had instead voted to ratify.
